sentences.text: Yes.
A friend, whom I had grown up with, he was part of our group.
He had family in London, whom he joined early.
And he was very-- he joined his uncle's business and was very comfortable.
When I was in London before emigrating, we got together and were the greatest friends, as we always had been.
When we came to London as immigrants and I called him, quite innocently just to say hello and get together with him, he would not come to the phone anymore.
He would not return my call.
He didn't want to know, know me anymore because he was afraid I might ask, might ask him for money.
So you learned a lot about people's real character in those days.
Never again.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And if I saw him, I would not be very gentle with him.
He is better off not seeing me.
It took about six months.
And finally, one day we got a call from Woburn House, which was the Jewish women's organization in London that took care of immigrants.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To tell-- well, I should say that we finally got our American immigration visas in those days.
And we told that to Woburn House, that we were ready to go.
And-- but then, because of the war, transportation was extremely scarce.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So it took a long time until one day, somebody from Woburn House called and said, we have a Japanese ship leaving tomorrow.
Are you ready to sail?
There was only room for Hella and myself, not for my parents.
So we said yes.
We are ready.
Got everything together in our house.
Said goodbye to our friends.
I should mention that I had a very good friend in England, an Englishman, whom I had met as a student in Munich.
We happened to rent our rooms from the same family.
We were next door to each other.
And we became the closest friends for life.
And he was the one who took the money that I had smuggled out of Germany and saw to it that it was well invested.
And we were in touch to his last day.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
So we sailed on the a Japanese ship.
Was it common to have Japanese ships?
No.
It was very unusual.
It was the only transportation available because that ship wanted to go to Japan-- home.
In those days, of course, Japan was still a neutral power.
It was not involved in the war, but the ship could not go through the Suez Canal, because of the war.
It had to go through the Panama Canal.
So it went to the United States, and that way, we got on that ship.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So it took two weeks.
It was a great advantage that it was a Japanese ship because it was not attacked by German U-boats.
So we didn't have to zigzag.
And we just went straight, but it still took two weeks because it was a slow ship.
Were there a lot of instances where ships would be attacked by--
Yes.
As a matter of fact, we could not leave the harbor of London on the night we were scheduled to sail because the Germans had sown mine all over the English Channel that, that very day.
So it took a full day for the mines to be cleared by the English minesweepers.
And then we finally set sail at night.
And the next day, we met our sister ship that was on its way to, to London, where we had just come from.
And a short time after we met, that ship hit a mine and sank.
We were--
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we must have passed that spot very closely where that ship had sunk because it happened shortly after we met.
Back in London, people didn't-- it was in the London papers that the had sunk.
And who knows the difference between one Japanese name and another?
So people thought that our ship had been sunk.
And they carefully called up my parents and said, everything all right?
Without coming right out to what they were afraid of.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So my parents knew what was going on and reassured them that it was a different ship that had hit a mine and sunk.
So it took two weeks to get to the United States.
I should mention that a cousin of mine, his wife, and his mother also were on that ship.
They had also waited in London and finally got their American visas.
They were on the ship, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a few other immigrants were on, on the same ship.
And it was also incongruous.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were well dressed on the ship.
And the night before we were supposed to land, there was the captain's dinner.
And we were invited to the captain's dinner.
And we were all dressed.
I had my dinner jacket because I happened to have a dinner jacket.
I packed it.
I never had any use for it afterwards.
So that evening, I was wearing my dinner jacket.
The next day, we were going to land in New York.
And I had no idea where the next penny was going to come from.
We were down to practically nothing by, by that time.
But it was a nice evening, only the captain didn't show up for a long time.
And finally he came, and he said, Nantucket, light ship, no could find, no could find.
It was very reassuring, but he did find New York Harbor after all the next day.
And we landed.
Passed the Statue of Liberty and landed in Hoboken, where we were picked up by a cousin of Hella's and his wife, the same cousin whom I had been to school with for eight years.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And his wife happened to be a very good friend of Hella's.
They had rented a furnished room for us uptown between Central Park West and Columbus Avenue.
It was a room right under the roof, and I think it cost $7 a week.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's where we spent the first year, almost, in in the, in the-- certainly in, in that building, but not in the same room.
Because during the-- one July day, it was exceptionally hot.
And air conditioning was only known in movies in those days.
Hella fainted because the heat under the roof.
And then we took a room in the same building on the ground floor.
Burglaries, you did not have to worry about in those days.
My parents rented-- came six months later.
They came in a convoy that had to zigzag its way over because of the German U-boats.
But they also made it over safely and rented a room in the same street in a different building.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Would you like me to talk about my experience in this country now?
Well, what was it--
Or yeah, what other questions?
No.
No.
Did Hella tell you about her job offer at the World's Fair?
Yes.
That was the only offer we had through a Jewish organization.
OK.
She was asked to be as scantily dressed as possible for the World's Fair of 1939, to stand at a-- outside a side show and attract people.
Hella looked very good and had a fabulous figure.
And it would have been very-- she would have been very decorative, I'm sure, but we finally decided not to do it.
It was left to her whether to accept it or not.
And reluctantly we said no because the pay was $50 a week or there about.
And that was a fortune.
In those days, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] friends of mine became employees at Klein's on the square.
That was a women's clothing store, which has long since disappeared.
And their job was to take the dresses with the hangers that-- well, the dresses that women had thrown on the floor after trying them on and putting them back on the hanger and on the racks.
And for that, they got, got $12 a week.
Now, if-- a week or so after we arrived, we were invited with relatives of relatives of relatives of mine.
A cousin of mine had-- and her husband had immigrated to the United States, I guess about a year, a year before we did, a year and a half.
And they lived in Fall River.
And they had relatives in New York, whom they notified about us.
We were invited by them for dinner.
And these people, unbeknownst to us, had invited another couple, a man, Charles Adler, who happened to be president of Wallachs, the men's stores.
They are just about disappearing.
Now the last store is on 45th Street and Fifth Avenue now.
And that is disappearing, too.
At that time, there were about eight stores all over New York City.
I had no idea who he was.
And if he had told me, I wouldn't have known either.
So he looked me over, and after dinner he said to me, would you like to work, to work for me?
I'm the president of Wallachs.
I said, oh, really, not knowing what it was, but jobs were extremely hard to get in those days.
It was the-- still the tail-end of the Depression.
And it was very difficult to get a job.
So I was delighted.
I didn't expect that, and I said, certainly.
So he said, report on Monday morning to the manager of the store, Mr.
Mann ?] in the Empire State Building.
That store occupied the good part of the ground floor of the Empire State Building, facing Fifth Avenue.
And it only disappeared a few years ago.
It's now a Duane Reade store.
So I reported, and I became a stock boy with the DHP.

[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I had to see that the shirts were properly lined up in the showcases, the ties were hanging well from the racks, and when a sale was made, I had to go downstairs, have it wrapped, and then I handed it to the customer.
How did you feel doing this sort of work?
Absolutely fine.
I was happy to have work, to earn $18 a week, which was a good salary.
And then came Christmas immediately because that was in October '39.
And they hired Hella for gift wrapping, just for the season.
And she became an expert wrapper to this day.
When she wraps a package, people think it comes straight from the store.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After six months, I became a junior salesman.
That means I didn't have my own sales book.
I had to write my sales into the sales book of a senior salesman.
After a few months, Mr.
Adler, the boss, called me in and he said, I need a supply buyer.
Would you like to do that?
Delighted.
I had to supply all-- buy all the supplies for the offices and the stores.
Well, it was printing matter or sweeping compound or typewriter ribbons or comptometers, which was-- were the adding machines and other computers in those days.
There was a constant clatter that the girls had to press keys.
I'm sure you've never heard of comptometers, but there were about a dozen in that one big room.
I had to buy any, any of these things.
And my salary went from $18 a week to $25 a week, which was princely.
We were terribly aware of what was happening, but without any details.
We did not know exactly what went on, what happened to our fellow Jews.
That-- just no word got out.
Until after the war.
In 1944, we had our son, Ralph, which was rather daring on our part because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wasn't making very much money.
Hella was working in those days, too, but between the two of us, we managed.
If it came to walking or taking a-- the subway or a bus for 10 blocks for a nickel, we walked.
We saved every penny.
Well, then I was called for the draft.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had to report together with a couple of friends of mine for a physical examination.
And the strangest thing happened.
I went through every doctor with flying colors until it came to the last one, who examined for hernia.
And he said, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] do you know you have a hernia?
I said, no, I didn't know it.
Go over and see the other doctor.
I went to the other doctor, and he said, you have a hernia.
I came back to the first one and he said, would you like to be drafted?
Which was a peculiar question.
I said, no.
He said-- so he put a stamp, 4-F, on the sheet.
And I was deferred [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because of a hernia that I never knew, knew existed.
After about a year and a half, I was re-examined.
The same thing happened again, except when I came to the last doctor, he looked and he, he examined me and he said, what were you rejected for?
A hernia?
You don't have a hernia.
And I became 1-A, ready to be drafted.
A few months after that, the war came to an end.
And I never served in the armed forces that way.
It turned out that the first doctor had been correct.
There, there is a hernia, which is now quite pronounced but in those days, probably wouldn't have harmed me in the, in the least.
So that was the story of my service.
The story came out very slowly.
We heard about it through word of mouth and mainly through, through the newspapers.
We did not know any more than what the general public was told through the newspapers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was an incredible experience.
And then, of course, slowly we found out that relatives of ours had been exterminated, killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were extremely fortunate that close relatives had not been affected, but parents of cousins of mine were.
One, two, three couples were sent to Riga.
And people in Riga were put into a concentration camp outside the city and were shot on short order.
They, they didn't last long.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another couple was killed right where they lived.
But it was not immediate family.
It was cousin-- parents of cousins, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of course, which was bad enough.
It was absolutely incredible.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was incredible that something like that happened.
And it was incredible to, to us that it was permitted to happen without interference.
We felt very strongly that something could have been done, that the concentration camp railroads could have bombed, that some help could have been rendered that was not.
It was really-- our feelings about Roosevelt, whom we had considered our savior until that time, were completely reversed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and about Churchill and the other leaders, too.
It really became a feeling of rage.
We went back to-- Hella and I went back to Germany-- I forgot the exact year-- for one reason only.
We wanted to look at the cemeteries.
So we went to Nuremberg, and I had the weirdest feeling.
Number one, the city has changed from when we lived there because it was bombed.
It was really completely destroyed and very well rebuilt, with some exceptions where suddenly a ultra-modern building stands between some very old, beautifully restored buildings.
But that is immaterial.
I had a feeling of not belonging in there.
I didn't want to see anybody.
I had no desire to see any of my former classmates.
So we did not call anybody.
We stayed at a hotel for, for one night and went to the two cemeteries in, in Nuremberg and got out again and did the same thing several years later.
There we went to-- first to several country cemeteries where our family had been buried in former years, going back to 1700.
And sadly, some of tombstones cannot be located anymore.
The inscription has been completely washed away and weathered.
And I'm still trying to identify some of them, get somebody to do it.
And we stayed-- again, we stayed for one night.
And, well one thing I must say, that one night was quite interesting.
Did Hella tell you about that?
We-- the city of Nuremberg, like so many other German cities, invites its former Jewish citizens for a stay of 10 days to two weeks, whatever it may be.
Most cities pay for the entire trip.
The city of Nuremberg just pays for the stay there.
The, the transportation is your own responsibility.
You pay for the trip.
But then they house you, feed you, even give you pocket money for 10 days and take you all over town.
You get a reception by the mayor of Nuremberg, and they show you all the sights and are extremely friendly and try-- there's one woman in charge of these affairs.
And she is a fabulous person.
And it so happened that our scheduled visit for the cemetery was-- coincided with the visit of the invitees.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we knew the program.
And--
Hella would.
I, I don't.
I'm not sure when it was.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one night, the, the group was invited for the opera in Nuremberg.
And it so happened that the opera given that night was one that I love and that is never performed in this country.
So I wrote to that woman in charge over there and asked if I-- if we could attend.
We would certainly pay for our tickets.
And she wrote back, you have two tickets free of charge.
You're free to attend all the affairs for the 10 days.
Only the hotel rooms are fully booked, and the money allocated for that is completely spoken for.
So, so we wrote back that we have no way and no intention of staying more than for one night, and we would be very grateful if we could attend the, the opera, which we did.
And that-- so we were there with the whole group that was invited for that, for those 10 days.
And that was quite pleasant.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was the best memory of Nuremberg after the war.
We went to where the temple had been.
And there's now a big plaque with an inscription.
And at-- on this spot stood the temple, destroyed by the National Socialists in 1938.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we visited the cemeteries, which are in good condition.
The cemeteries in Germany are maintained by the state, by the individual states, in our case, the state of Bavaria in Munich.
They don't take care of the individual graves, but of the cemetery in, in general.
No.
Did not affect my belief.
It only affected my way of looking at Germans and mankind in particular.
Well, I just don't want to have anything to do with the German generation that was old-- that is old enough to have, have participated in these events.
When we meet a younger German couple that was born after the war or were small children during the war, that, that is different.
In fact, we met one couple on a vacation who we became so friendly with.
They, they came over here, and we were together.
And we correspond to this day.
But the older generation, we don't have-- we don't want to have anything to do with.
Yes.
The Blue Card, I'm, I'm the president of Blue Card.
I have been for about eight or 10 years now.
The Blue Card is a charitable organization that helps former refugees from Europe, up until the Russian border, all the way from France to Poland, including Poland.
The Jews who came over here and are without means or became indigent, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we help with cash.
Other organizations, of course, exist, and they help with services, social services.
But we help with cash.
If somebody can't pay the doctor or for-- or the rent or [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the groceries, we, we give them actual cash.
I retired on April 1.
It was rather late, but I was in good shape.
And I think I did a creditable job until the end.
In the end, I was-- took care of the sales of a firm that manufactures boxes for packaging, transparent boxes, cardboard boxes.
The factory is in Philadelphia, and I took care of the New York territory.
